Subject: Depression position finders, on tripods
Subject Details: Optical instruments designed to calculate the range to a target (typically a ship) by measuring the angle of depression from a known height above sea level. Key components of early vertical-base fire-control systems in coastal artillery and base-end stations.
Proposer Details: William Carroll Rafferty (1859 - 1941) graduated from West Point in 1880 and rose to Brigadier General. He was also on the Board on Regulation of Coast Artillery Fire (1894–1897), placing him directly in line with fire-control innovations.
Recommendation Reasoning: This proposal came from someone directly involved in shaping U.S. coastal defense strategy and fire control. Subject: Instrument for observation of fire
Proposed By: Board on Regulation of Seacoast Artillery Fire.
Subject Details: An optical/mechanical observation instrument used in coastal artillery fire-control systems to spot and relay information on the fall of shot (splashes). Observers would track projectile impact deviations in range or azimuth and transmit corrections to plotting rooms, supporting rapid adjustment of aiming data via plotting boards, deflection boards, and range correction tools. These systems were core components of U.S. Coast Artillery fire-control systems and remained in use from the late 1890s through World War II.
Proposer Details: The Board on the Regulation of Seacoast Artillery Fire was a U.S. Army body tasked with standardizing coastal defense practices in the late 1890s. Its responsibilities included revising the Drill Regulations for Coast Artillery, calculating range tables and fire-control scales for coastal guns, and reviewing submitted military devices. The Board also supervised the outfitting of a model artillery station at Fort Wadsworth, though full testing was delayed due to members' active field duties.
Recommendation Reasoning: Fire-control instrumentation was foundational to modern coastal defense. Understanding its evolution helps trace how target acquisition progressed before radar.

Subject: Aerodrome
Subject Details:
S. P. Langley began developing airplane prototypes, which are all called "Langley Aerodrome" in the 1890s:
1894-1896- first prototype testing-unsuccessful unmanned flights, all end up crashing
1896- first successful unmanned flight over the Potomac (lasts 90 seconds)
1898- Assistant Secretary of the Navy Theodore Roosevelt commissions Navy to support the project
1899- Board of Ordnance gives Langley 50,000$ grant
1903- 2 unsuccessful manned flights over Potomac, overtaken by Wright Brothers airplane


Despite a short revival in 1913, the Langley aerodrome was discontinued and never flew again. It had serious engineering flaws, such as the fact that it had no steering system and was incredibly unstable and hard to maneuver. It became the subject of ridicule in Washington soon after it’s failed test flights.
Proposer Details: The Langley Aeroplane was designed, and built by engineer, astronomer, and physicist Samuel Langley. An accomplished astronomer, he served as the director of Alleghany Observatory and Proffesor of Astronomy at the University of Pittsburgh, he most notably served as the 3rd Secretrary of the Smithsonian Institution. He was given a joint grant (70,000$) from the War Department and the Smithsonian to develop the aerodrome. Langley died of a stroke soon after the end of the project in 1905.
Recommendation Reasoning: The Aerodrome was a significant step in developing the modern airplane and many of its components doubtlessly inspired the Wright brothers in their successful design. It’s collaboration with the Ordnance Board is certainly worthy of study

Subject: Squior Crehore range finder
Proposed By:
G. O. Squier was a US Army General who was also a prolific inventor. He received a PhD. from John Hopkins University in 1893. He wrote multiple volumes about radio and electricity and was later instrumental in founding the US Army Signal Corps Aeuronauticla division, during which he negotiated the first ever purchase of airplanes for military purpose, from the Wright Brothers, in 1908. He became the US Army's Chief Signal Officer during WW1.

A. C. Crehore was an American engineer. After graduating from Yale, he studied at Johns Hopkins before getting a PhD in physics from Cornell. He started his career by pioneering quantum physics. After is business venture with Squier failed, he disappeared for nearly 30 years until he was discovered living as a hermit in a shack in Cleveland, Ohio. In the meanwhile, his theories gained widespread acclaim and became the basis for modern quantum mechanics theory. Before dying in 1958, he was commissioned by the US Government to lead the development of the world’s first ICBM.
Subject Details: One of the world's first electrical range finders. Board gave the project multiple allotments and it was tested extensively at Sandy Hook. In the end, it was deemed to impractical and fragile and was not used. (note: typographical error in original text, real name of inventor is Squier not Soffor)

Recommendation Reasoning: Despite the failure of this particular project, the two inventors went on to have spectacularly impactful careers and they are definitely worthy of deeper research as part of their collaboration with the Board of Ordnance.

proposer_text:
G. O. Squier was a US Army General who was also a prolific inventor. He received a PhD. from John Hopkins University in 1893. He wrote multiple volumes about radio and electricity and was later instrumental in founding the US Army Signal Corps Aeuronauticla division, during which he negotiated the first ever purchase of airplanes for military purpose, from the Wright Brothers, in 1908. He became the US Army's Chief Signal Officer during WW1.

A. C. Crehore was an American engineer. After graduating from Yale, he studied at Johns Hopkins before getting a PhD in physics from Cornell. He started his career by pioneering quantum physics. After is business venture with Squier failed, he disappeared for nearly 30 years until he was discovered living as a hermit in a shack in Cleveland, Ohio. In the meanwhile, his theories gained widespread acclaim and became the basis for modern quantum mechanics theory. Before dying in 1958, he was commissioned by the US Government to lead the development of the world’s first ICBM.

Subject: Joveite
Subject Details: In the 1890s, the world's militaries attempted to systematically replace dynamite as their main explosive, which was seen as too unstable and dangerous. Joveite was one of the explosives considered. It was considerably more stable than dynamite but with low explosive power. It was publicly tested in 1895 and tested by the Board in 1899, but was found unsuitable. It faded into obscurity soon after.
Proposer Details: Internal submission for testing by the Chief of Ordnance. No inventor is know and the mixture was never patented.
Recommendation Reasoning: New explosives were one of the priorities of the Board of Ordnance. Despite it's failure, Joveite could be a very interesting case study for how the Board handled emerging tech.

Subject: Bufington-Crozier 10-inch disappearing carriage
Action:
To be tested at Galveston,
Tex.
Subject Details:
This 10-inch coast defense disappearing (it had a mechanism which allowed it to protect itself by hiding behind a parapet)  gun was developed internally, exensively test by the Board, and entered widespread usage in the United States. It is one of the most succesful creations of the Board and was used for coastal defences up until the end of WW2. Nine 10-inch guns (one partial) remain at four locations: 
1. Two 10-inch Guns M1895MI (#25 & #22 Watervliet) on Disappearing Carriages M1901 (#14 & #16 Watertown), Battery Grubbs, Fort Mills, Corregidor Island, Philippines. The guns lie behind their mountings, since they were fired while disconnected from the carriages to deny use of them to the Japanese forces.

2. One 10-inch Gun M1895MI (#20 Watervliet) (spare gun), Battery Grubbs, Fort Mills, Corregidor Island, Philippines

3. Two 10-inch Guns M1895MI (#26 & #28 Watervliet) on Disappearing Carriages M1901 (#13 & #15 Watertown), Battery Worth, Fort Casey, Coupeville, WA (guns moved in the 1960s from Battery Warwick, Fort Wint, Grande Island, Subic Bay, Philippines)

4. Two 10-inch Guns M1888 (#41 & #3 Watervliet), Fort Cape Spear, St. John's, Newfoundland, Canada (guns moved in World War II from Battery Harker, Fort Mott, New Jersey)

5. One 10-inch Gun M1888 (#12 Watervliet) on Barbette Carriage M1893 (#11 Watertown) (with partial remains of other gun (#37) and carriage (#1)), Fort McNutt, McNutts Island, Nova Scotia, Canada (guns moved in World War II from Battery Quarles, Fort Worden, WA)
Proposer Details: Buffington and Crozier were distinquished ordnance/artillery officers, both of whom served as Chiefs of Ordnance of the US Army. William Crozier is credited with transforming the US Army into a modern force by extensively reforming ordnance procurement and leading the adoption of new weapons, such as the M1911 Colt, M1903 Springfield, and 1917 Brown machine gun.
Recommendation Reasoning: Being one the most successful products of the Board of Ordance, which was used internationally for decades to come, it is definitely worthy of further research.

Subject: Airship
Subject Details:
-There were no less than 45 different "Airship" designs submmitted to the Board of Ordnance in the years 1898-1899 (in blue). They represent 24.3% of all submissions. Apart from the Langley aeroplane, all of them were rejected. Here are some observations:

-Airplane arms race: Pre-WW1, the western world experienced an arms race to develop the first functional airplane. While the Wright Brothers ultimately won this race on December 17, 1903, many other prototypes were simultaneously developed. The surge in aerospace research activity is very obvious in the research report and the sheer volume of unsuccessful prototypes is quite interesting
-Grass-roots process: except Langley's model, all of the submissions came from local engineers/inventors who lacked formal education. This trend shows that the Board of Ordnance was a surprisingly democratic, as it received submissions from inventors outside of the military and academia. This mirrors the Navy’s WW1 Board, which also reviewed thousands of submissions from non-professional backgrounds.

-More particularly, the Board itself states that the volume is likely a result of a member of the Board leaking the allotment given to S. P. Langley for the development of the well known Langley aeroplane:

“It will be noted that there has been a marked increase in the number of flying machines, aerodromes, and other similar devices designed for air navigation. This is no doubt due to the unauthorized publication in the daily press, some months ago, of the fact that the Board had made an allotment of funds to enable Mr. S. P. Langley, Secretary of the Smithsonian Institution, to personally conduct a series of practical experiments in this line of miilitary investigation.
Recommendation Reasoning: There is a lack of available information on these models. In addition, despite being interesting, the models were likely not very well designed
reasoning_text: There is a lack of available information on these models. In addition, despite being interesting, the models were likely not very well designed

Subject: Thorite
Subject Details: Very interesting invention. As the US Army/Navy was looking for an explosive to replace dynamite, it tested many alternatives. The most promising one ended up being Thorite,(not be confused with the element thorium of the periodic table). Being a nitrate mixture, it was more stable and reliable than dynamite, while still retaining explosive capacity. It was extensively tested at Sandy Hook, however, it was not adopted in the end and fell into obscurity. Outside of Navy Board of Ordance Documents and one news report, there is no other mention of this substance.
Proposer Details: H. P. Tuttle was an astronomer, Civil War Veteran, and astronomer. After serving in the Civil War, he became an avid comet-seeker. Not much else is known about him.
Recommendation Reasoning: Fascinating obscure explosive that was completely forgotten for over 100 years.
reasoning_text: Fascinating obscure explosive that was completely forgotten for over 100 years.

Subject: Facilities for manufacture of optical instruments
Subject Details: Funding for development of domestic optics company Warner and Swasey. Likely in order to offset dependence on foreign optical imports for military usage (binoculars, sights, telescopes). Can be said to be a 19th century form of industrial policy.
Proposer Details: Warner and Swasey was founded in 1880 in Cleveland, Ohio. It's focus was on producing milling machines and telescopes, the latter of which peaked the interest of the US Navy, which became a regular customer. Bendix Corporation bought the company in 1980.

Subject: Isham shell and Tuttle thorite, purchase of
Action: Purchase of Isham shell not recommended at this time; compensation for Dr. Tuttle recommended.
Subject Details: The Isham shell was an experimental artillery projectile proposed for Army adoption but not approved; Tuttle thorite, an explosive designed by Dr. Horace Tuttle, was deemed worthy of compensation by the Board.
Proposer Details: The proposal came through the Secretary of War, who forwarded the matter to the Board of Ordnance and Fortification. The Isham shell was attributed to a private inventor (Isham), though little else is documented about his other contributions. The explosive thorite was developed by Dr. Horace Tuttle, an inventor and chemist who filed patents on chemical compounds in the late 19th century. While Tuttle’s name is not well known in later industrial or defense development, his work is an example of private inventors pushing novel explosives to the U.S. military during a period of rapid experimentation. The Board ultimately recommended compensating Dr. Tuttle for his efforts, which shows official acknowledgment of his contribution, even if the technology was not adopted.
Recommendation Reasoning: This case is valuable because it illustrates the U.S. Army’s engagement with experimental munitions and explosives at a pivotal moment in artillery development. While the Isham shell itself went nowhere, thorite fits into the larger story of high-explosive chemistry

Subject: High explosives, all tests of
Action: Recommended that they be conducted by the board, as contemplated by law.
Subject Details: High explosives like dynamite, guncotton, and picric acid were being tested to replace black powder with stronger, more reliable charges. The idea was to find a safer and more powerful explosive for artillery and demolition. This was not a foreign import but part of wider experiments happening in Europe at the same time. The Army conducted trials at Sandy Hook, but only TNT went on to be widely adopted later. No surviving “Isham” or thorite shells exist, though reports remain in archives.
Proposer Details: The proposal came from the Board of Ordnance and Fortification itself. The Board’s role was to standardize explosive testing as required by law. This ensured experiments were centralized under the War Department rather than scattered across inventors and posts. The Board later became the key filter for deciding which new explosives merited further investment.
Recommendation Reasoning: This subject shaped the entire Army approach to new explosives and ties U.S. trials to global military innovation.

Subject: Test of Bofors gun
Subject Details: U.S. Army made provisions to test a Bofors 40 mm anti-aircraft autocannon in late 1940. This was a foreign design from Sweden, not a domestic invention. The tests aimed to find a more effective medium-caliber AA gun than the standard 37 mm. The Bofors gun was soon tested at Aberdeen and Fort Monroe by Army and Navy officers. It proved superior and eventually became the U.S. standard AA weapon as the 40 mm Automatic Gun M1. Examples exist in historical collections and many remain at military museums.
Recommendation Reasoning: This is a major milestone in U.S. military adoption, moving from 37 mm to the iconic 40 mm Bofors -- connects foreign innovation, inter-service collaboration, and U.S. industrial scaling.

Subject: Gruson turrets
Subject Details: Gruson Iron Works in Germany produced cast-iron armored turrets—curved, chilled-cast cupolas—for coast-defense fortifications. These rotating turrets enveloped large guns with only small slits for firing. They addressed the problem of protecting artillery crews from high-angle explosive fire.  Few of these structures survive intact; some turreted fortifications exist in Europe, and one converted turret is on display in Fort Dix, New Jersey.
Proposer Details: Gruson Iron Works (later part of Krupp after 1892) was a major German armament firm. The U.S.’s role was observational and evaluative—a “filed” entry indicating plans to examine them further, but no procurement followed.
Recommendation Reasoning: provides insight into the early U.S. Army’s assessment of European fortification technologies
reasoning_text: provides insight into the early U.S. Army’s assessment of European fortification technologies

Subject: Electrical firing devices in test of gun
Subject Details: Brown & Munsell proposed using electrical firing devices for artillery testing, replacing or supplementing mechanical primers. The goal was more precise ignition timing and safer operation during controlled trials. This was a new application of electrical systems, part of wider experimentation in the late 19th–early 20th century. Permission was granted for use, showing the Army’s willingness to trial modern ignition methods. Electrical firing later became standard in naval gunnery and test ranges, though not widely used for field artillery at this time. No surviving “Brown & Munsell” devices are identified, but the concept evolved into modern electronic primers and remote firing circuits.
Proposer Details: Brown & Munsell, an engineering and ordnance contracting firm, submitted the firing devices. Beyond this listing, little is documented about their broader role in ordnance innovation. Their contribution here fits into a wider wave of private firms offering experimental equipment to the Board.
Recommendation Reasoning: This connects directly to a broader technological shift—electrical firing in heavy guns and test ranges. Even if Brown & Munsell themselves are obscure, the subject ties into the modernization of ordnance practice and later naval/field gunnery.

Subject: 75 mm. field gun, description of
Subject Details: The 75 mm field gun offered by Vickers, Sons & Maxim was a quick-firing artillery piece in the same class as the famous French Canon de 75 modèle 1897. The purpose was to give the U.S. Army access to modern, rapid-fire field artillery with a hydro-pneumatic recoil system that allowed sustained, accurate fire without re-laying the gun. This was a foreign design, imported for evaluation, not an American invention. The Board filed the description, showing interest but not immediate adoption. The U.S. ultimately standardized other models, but trials of 75 mm systems influenced later domestic designs. Surviving examples of Vickers and other foreign 75 mm pieces exist in museums like the U.S. Army Artillery Museum (Fort Sill).
Proposer Details: Vickers, Sons & Maxim, a British arms manufacturer, was one of the world’s leading producers of artillery and machine guns at the turn of the 20th century. Known for their Maxim machine guns and naval ordnance, they competed globally with Krupp and Schneider. Their submission of a 75 mm gun to the U.S. Army reflects efforts to win export markets during the rapid modernization of artillery.
Recommendation Reasoning: The 75 mm caliber became the standard modern field gun size in the early 20th century. Even if this Vickers model was not adopted, studying it alongside French, German, and later U.S. 75 mm systems provides crucial comparative context for understanding artillery modernization.

Subject: Wireless telegraphy
Subject Details: Wireless telegraphy refers to early radio communication by spark-gap transmitters and coherer receivers, pioneered in the 1890s by Guglielmo Marconi, Oliver Lodge, and others. By 1901–1902, armies and navies worldwide were experimenting with this technology for tactical signaling. Werner’s proposal reflects the U.S. Army’s interest in applying wireless to artillery and coastal defense communications. The Board marked it “not recommended”, suggesting his system did not meet performance standards or duplicated other ongoing experiments. Though Werner’s design left no trace, the larger field advanced rapidly: the U.S. Army Signal Corps formally adopted wireless sets beginning in 1902–1903, with systems by Fessenden and De Forest. Surviving examples of early military wireless equipment are preserved in the Smithsonian’s National Museum of American History and the U.S. Army Signal Corps Museum.
Proposer Details: L. C. Werner is recorded as submitting a wireless telegraphy scheme to the Board of Ordnance and Fortification. No further archival records, patents, or professional affiliations have been traced under his name, suggesting he was an independent experimenter or small contractor.
Recommendation Reasoning: broader story of how the U.S. evaluated early wireless
reasoning_text: broader story of how the U.S. evaluated early wireless

Subject: Brown wire-wound gun, further test
Subject Details:
refers to an artillery piece whose barrel was constructed with layers of high-tensile steel wire wound under tension around an inner tube. This method, developed in Britain in the late 19th century (notably by Sir William Armstrong), was designed to strengthen the gun barrel against the immense pressures of firing while reducing overall weight. Brown & Munsell’s submission reflects an American attempt to compete with or adapt this technology.

The entry “further test granted” in the Board of Ordnance and Fortification records (1901–1902) shows that the U.S. was willing to continue evaluating the design after initial trials. However, wire-wound construction never became standard in U.S. service. By the 1910s, built-up guns with autofrettaged monobloc barrels were judged superior, and wire-wound methods were largely abandoned except in Britain.
Proposer Details: Brown & Munsell were American contractors/manufacturers who presented ordnance designs to the Board during this period. Little is recorded about them outside of the BOF listings

Subject: Shields and mounts for 6-pounder guns
Subject Details: The 6-pounder rapid-fire gun was widely used by the U.S. Army and Navy around 1900, both in coast defense and aboard ships. As quick-firing weapons became standard, gunners were increasingly exposed to shrapnel and small-arms fire. To address this, the Artillery Committee reviewed designs for protective shields and revised mounts to absorb recoil and allow more flexible traverse. Shields were a modification of existing systems, following European practice (Krupp and Schneider were already using gun shields). U.S. boards inspected and recommended various shielded mounts for emplacement at coastal batteries and with field detachments. Surviving examples of shielded 6-pounders can be found in U.S. coastal forts and naval museums.
Proposer Details: Proposed internally by the Artillery Committee, a body of senior officers tasked with evaluating technical improvements for field and coast artillery.
Recommendation Reasoning: valuable for connecting U.S. practice to wider global trends in artillery protection
reasoning_text: valuable for connecting U.S. practice to wider global trends in artillery protection

Subject: War balloon
Subject Details:
continuing interest in lighter-than-air craft for military reconnaissance and observation at the turn of the 20th century. Balloons had been used in the American Civil War and by European armies (notably France, Germany, and Britain) for artillery spotting and intelligence. By 1901–1902, the U.S. Army Signal Corps was experimenting with captive observation balloons at Fort Myer, while navies abroad explored dirigibles.

Freid’s “war balloon” proposal was filed with the Board but did not result in procurement or testing. It was not a new concept but rather a modification of an established practice. The Army soon shifted attention from balloons to powered dirigibles and then to heavier-than-air craft. Surviving contemporary balloons are preserved in photographs and equipment records at the U.S. Army Signal Corps Museum.
Proposer Details: C. T. Freid submitted the concept to the Board. Beyond the listing in the Serial Set, there is no traceable record of his background in ordnance or aeronautics.

Subject: Armstrong field-gun carriage
Subject Details: The Armstrong field-gun carriage referred to a trial of Armstrong, Whitworth & Co. (UK) artillery equipment—part of the global push to modernize field guns with recoil control and improved mobility. Armstrong’s quick-firing carriages incorporated hydro-spring recoil systems and were paired with small-caliber QF guns (6–12 pounders) for use by several European armies. The U.S. Board of Ordnance and Fortification evaluated this carriage to compare it with domestic designs. The action “inspected” suggests the Board examined it but did not adopt it. The U.S. instead developed the 3-inch M1902 field gun, but Armstrong’s designs provided an important benchmark. Surviving Armstrong QF field guns with their carriages are preserved at the Royal Armouries (UK) and in military museums in Japan and Italy.
Proposer Details: Capt. Edmund Louis Zalinski (1849–1909), U.S. Army artillery officer and inventor, submitted the Armstrong carriage for Board evaluation. Zalinski was already known for his invention of the pneumatic dynamite gun in the 1880s, which had been deployed in U.S. coastal defenses. By 1901–1902, he served on or consulted with the Board, sponsoring foreign designs for comparative trials. Although the dynamite gun was later declared obsolete, Zalinski remained an influential figure in experimental ordnance.
Recommendation Reasoning: how the U.S. benchmarked its artillery against leading foreign manufacturers

Subject: Pouch for first-aid packet
Subject Details: A pouch meant to carry the first-aid packet—typically the Carlisle dressing (a linen gauze pad with long tails in a sealed brass casing), standard U.S. Army issue since around 1906. It addressed the need for immediate wound dressing in the field, tied to the broader move from black powder-era logistics to modern battlefield medical readiness. This was a modification of existing medical kit design, not a novel concept or foreign import.
Proposer Details: Proposed by Maj. J. Van R. Hoff, presumably serving in the U.S. Army Medical Department around the early 1900s. He probably recommended appropriating funds or allotting resources for testing this pouch design.

Subject: Mountain gun
Subject Details: A mountain gun is a lightweight artillery piece designed for use in rugged terrain where traditional guns aren’t practical. In this case, Bethlehem Steel Co. developed a 3-inch Mountain Gun, Model of 1917, intended for such conditions. It was a domestic modification tailored to U.S. Army needs. The U.S. Army made provisions to test it, indicating interest—but it did not enter widespread service. A surviving photograph from September 1918 confirms the model existed.
Proposer Details: Proposed by Bethlehem Steel Company, a major U.S. arms manufacturer in the early 20th century. They were well-known for producing large-caliber guns, howitzers, and forgings for military use. This mountain gun reflects their efforts to diversify artillery offerings. While Bethlehem Steel had broad influence—e.g., M1902 3-inch seacoast guns and M1908 6-inch howitzers—the mountain gun was a less successful niche instrument.
Recommendation Reasoning: small-scale experiment with limited adoption. Unless further documentation surfaces, it’s probably not worth deep investigation relative to more impactful entries.

Subject: Nonfulminate fuse
Subject Details: Nonfulminate fuses were proposed as safer, more stable alternatives to traditional mercury fulminate–based detonators, which were highly sensitive to shock and temperature—posing risks during storage, handling, and transport. These newer fuses aimed to replace unstable fulminates with compounds like lead azide or lead styphnate, marking a significant shift in detonation safety. Although Prof. F. Ant. Hubbuch’s design was not adopted, it aligned with a gradual global move toward safer primary explosives—a change that laid the foundation for modern military detonators.
Proposer Details: Prof. F. Ant. Hubbuch appears in Board records as the proposer of a nonfulminate fuse. Subject: Isham shell and Tuttle thorite, purchase of
Action: Purchase of Isham shell not recommended at this time; compensation for Dr. Tuttle recommended.
Subject Details: The Isham shell was an experimental artillery projectile proposed for Army adoption but not approved; Tuttle thorite, an explosive designed by Dr. Horace Tuttle, was deemed worthy of compensation by the Board.
Proposer Details: The proposal came through the Secretary of War, who forwarded the matter to the Board of Ordnance and Fortification. The Isham shell was attributed to a private inventor (Isham), though little else is documented about his other contributions. The explosive thorite was developed by Dr. Horace Tuttle, an inventor and chemist who filed patents on chemical compounds in the late 19th century. While Tuttle’s name is not well known in later industrial or defense development, his work is an example of private inventors pushing novel explosives to the U.S. military during a period of rapid experimentation. The Board ultimately recommended compensating Dr. Tuttle for his efforts, which shows official acknowledgment of his contribution, even if the technology was not adopted.
Recommendation Reasoning: This case is valuable because it illustrates the U.S. Army’s engagement with experimental munitions and explosives at a pivotal moment in artillery development. While the Isham shell itself went nowhere, thorite fits into the larger story of high-explosive chemistry

Subject: High explosives, all tests of
Action: Recommended that they be conducted by the board, as contemplated by law.
Subject Details: High explosives like dynamite, guncotton, and picric acid were being tested to replace black powder with stronger, more reliable charges. The idea was to find a safer and more powerful explosive for artillery and demolition. This was not a foreign import but part of wider experiments happening in Europe at the same time. The Army conducted trials at Sandy Hook, but only TNT went on to be widely adopted later. No surviving “Isham” or thorite shells exist, though reports remain in archives.
Proposer Details: The proposal came from the Board of Ordnance and Fortification itself. The Board’s role was to standardize explosive testing as required by law. This ensured experiments were centralized under the War Department rather than scattered across inventors and posts. The Board later became the key filter for deciding which new explosives merited further investment.
Recommendation Reasoning: This subject shaped the entire Army approach to new explosives and ties U.S. trials to global military innovation.

Subject: Test of Bofors gun
Subject Details: U.S. Army made provisions to test a Bofors 40 mm anti-aircraft autocannon in late 1940. This was a foreign design from Sweden, not a domestic invention. The tests aimed to find a more effective medium-caliber AA gun than the standard 37 mm. The Bofors gun was soon tested at Aberdeen and Fort Monroe by Army and Navy officers. It proved superior and eventually became the U.S. standard AA weapon as the 40 mm Automatic Gun M1. Examples exist in historical collections and many remain at military museums.
Recommendation Reasoning: This is a major milestone in U.S. military adoption, moving from 37 mm to the iconic 40 mm Bofors -- connects foreign innovation, inter-service collaboration, and U.S. industrial scaling.
reasoning_text: This is a major milestone in U.S. military adoption, moving from 37 mm to the iconic 40 mm Bofors -- connects foreign innovation, inter-service collaboration, and U.S. industrial scaling.

Subject: Gruson turrets
Subject Details: Gruson Iron Works in Germany produced cast-iron armored turrets—curved, chilled-cast cupolas—for coast-defense fortifications. These rotating turrets enveloped large guns with only small slits for firing. They addressed the problem of protecting artillery crews from high-angle explosive fire.  Few of these structures survive intact; some turreted fortifications exist in Europe, and one converted turret is on display in Fort Dix, New Jersey.
Proposer Details: Gruson Iron Works (later part of Krupp after 1892) was a major German armament firm. The U.S.’s role was observational and evaluative—a “filed” entry indicating plans to examine them further, but no procurement followed.
Recommendation Reasoning: provides insight into the early U.S. Army’s assessment of European fortification technologies
reasoning_text: provides insight into the early U.S. Army’s assessment of European fortification technologies

Subject: Electrical firing devices in test of gun
Subject Details: Brown & Munsell proposed using electrical firing devices for artillery testing, replacing or supplementing mechanical primers. The goal was more precise ignition timing and safer operation during controlled trials. This was a new application of electrical systems, part of wider experimentation in the late 19th–early 20th century. Permission was granted for use, showing the Army’s willingness to trial modern ignition methods. Electrical firing later became standard in naval gunnery and test ranges, though not widely used for field artillery at this time. No surviving “Brown & Munsell” devices are identified, but the concept evolved into modern electronic primers and remote firing circuits.
Proposer Details: Brown & Munsell, an engineering and ordnance contracting firm, submitted the firing devices. Beyond this listing, little is documented about their broader role in ordnance innovation. Their contribution here fits into a wider wave of private firms offering experimental equipment to the Board.
Recommendation Reasoning: This connects directly to a broader technological shift—electrical firing in heavy guns and test ranges. Even if Brown & Munsell themselves are obscure, the subject ties into the modernization of ordnance practice and later naval/field gunnery.

Subject: 75 mm. field gun, description of
Subject Details: The 75 mm field gun offered by Vickers, Sons & Maxim was a quick-firing artillery piece in the same class as the famous French Canon de 75 modèle 1897. The purpose was to give the U.S. Army access to modern, rapid-fire field artillery with a hydro-pneumatic recoil system that allowed sustained, accurate fire without re-laying the gun. This was a foreign design, imported for evaluation, not an American invention. The Board filed the description, showing interest but not immediate adoption. The U.S. ultimately standardized other models, but trials of 75 mm systems influenced later domestic designs. Surviving examples of Vickers and other foreign 75 mm pieces exist in museums like the U.S. Army Artillery Museum (Fort Sill).
Proposer Details: Vickers, Sons & Maxim, a British arms manufacturer, was one of the world’s leading producers of artillery and machine guns at the turn of the 20th century. Known for their Maxim machine guns and naval ordnance, they competed globally with Krupp and Schneider. Their submission of a 75 mm gun to the U.S. Army reflects efforts to win export markets during the rapid modernization of artillery.
Recommendation Reasoning: The 75 mm caliber became the standard modern field gun size in the early 20th century. Even if this Vickers model was not adopted, studying it alongside French, German, and later U.S. 75 mm systems provides crucial comparative context for understanding artillery modernization.
reasoning_text: The 75 mm caliber became the standard modern field gun size in the early 20th century. Even if this Vickers model was not adopted, studying it alongside French, German, and later U.S. 75 mm systems provides crucial comparative context for understanding artillery modernization.

Subject Details: This entry refers to an early concept of combining artillery ("cannon") with an airship-launched torpedo, perhaps a variant of the aerial torpedo or guided munitions developed for dirigibles. The idea was innovative and forward-looking, aiming to extend projectile delivery via airship platforms. Though the U.S. Army filed the proposal, there's no indication it was tested or adopted. This concept aligns with early 20th-century notions of air-to-surface weapon delivery, akin to later experiments in aerial torpedoes. No surviving examples are known.

Subject: Wireless telegraphy
Subject Details: Wireless telegraphy refers to early radio communication by spark-gap transmitters and coherer receivers, pioneered in the 1890s by Guglielmo Marconi, Oliver Lodge, and others. By 1901–1902, armies and navies worldwide were experimenting with this technology for tactical signaling. Werner’s proposal reflects the U.S. Army’s interest in applying wireless to artillery and coastal defense communications. The Board marked it “not recommended”, suggesting his system did not meet performance standards or duplicated other ongoing experiments. Though Werner’s design left no trace, the larger field advanced rapidly: the U.S. Army Signal Corps formally adopted wireless sets beginning in 1902–1903, with systems by Fessenden and De Forest. Surviving examples of early military wireless equipment are preserved in the Smithsonian’s National Museum of American History and the U.S. Army Signal Corps Museum.
Proposer Details: L. C. Werner is recorded as submitting a wireless telegraphy scheme to the Board of Ordnance and Fortification. No further archival records, patents, or professional affiliations have been traced under his name, suggesting he was an independent experimenter or small contractor.
Recommendation Reasoning: broader story of how the U.S. evaluated early wireless
reasoning_text: broader story of how the U.S. evaluated early wireless

Subject: Brown wire-wound gun, further test
Subject Details:
refers to an artillery piece whose barrel was constructed with layers of high-tensile steel wire wound under tension around an inner tube. This method, developed in Britain in the late 19th century (notably by Sir William Armstrong), was designed to strengthen the gun barrel against the immense pressures of firing while reducing overall weight. Brown & Munsell’s submission reflects an American attempt to compete with or adapt this technology.

The entry “further test granted” in the Board of Ordnance and Fortification records (1901–1902) shows that the U.S. was willing to continue evaluating the design after initial trials. However, wire-wound construction never became standard in U.S. service. By the 1910s, built-up guns with autofrettaged monobloc barrels were judged superior, and wire-wound methods were largely abandoned except in Britain.
Proposer Details: Brown & Munsell were American contractors/manufacturers who presented ordnance designs to the Board during this period. Little is recorded about them outside of the BOF listings

Subject: Shields and mounts for 6-pounder guns
Subject Details: The 6-pounder rapid-fire gun was widely used by the U.S. Army and Navy around 1900, both in coast defense and aboard ships. As quick-firing weapons became standard, gunners were increasingly exposed to shrapnel and small-arms fire. To address this, the Artillery Committee reviewed designs for protective shields and revised mounts to absorb recoil and allow more flexible traverse. Shields were a modification of existing systems, following European practice (Krupp and Schneider were already using gun shields). U.S. boards inspected and recommended various shielded mounts for emplacement at coastal batteries and with field detachments. Surviving examples of shielded 6-pounders can be found in U.S. coastal forts and naval museums.
Proposer Details: Proposed internally by the Artillery Committee, a body of senior officers tasked with evaluating technical improvements for field and coast artillery.
Recommendation Reasoning: valuable for connecting U.S. practice to wider global trends in artillery protection
reasoning_text: valuable for connecting U.S. practice to wider global trends in artillery protection

Subject: Mauser automatic pistol, further trial of
Subject Details: The Mauser automatic pistol in question was almost certainly the Mauser C96 “Broomhandle”, introduced in 1896. This was one of the first successful semi-automatic pistols, chambered in 7.63×25 mm, with an integral 10-round magazine loaded by stripper clips. The Board of Ordnance and Fortification had already received samples for evaluation, and the note “further trial” indicates continued interest by 1901–1902.
Proposer Details: Cadet at the Military Academy, July 1, 1875, to June 13, 1879, when he was graduated and promoted in the Army

Subject: War balloon
Subject Details:
continuing interest in lighter-than-air craft for military reconnaissance and observation at the turn of the 20th century. Balloons had been used in the American Civil War and by European armies (notably France, Germany, and Britain) for artillery spotting and intelligence. By 1901–1902, the U.S. Army Signal Corps was experimenting with captive observation balloons at Fort Myer, while navies abroad explored dirigibles.

Freid’s “war balloon” proposal was filed with the Board but did not result in procurement or testing. It was not a new concept but rather a modification of an established practice. The Army soon shifted attention from balloons to powered dirigibles and then to heavier-than-air craft. Surviving contemporary balloons are preserved in photographs and equipment records at the U.S. Army Signal Corps Museum.
Proposer Details: C. T. Freid submitted the concept to the Board. Beyond the listing in the Serial Set, there is no traceable record of his background in ordnance or aeronautics.

Subject: Armstrong field-gun carriage
Subject Details: The Armstrong field-gun carriage referred to a trial of Armstrong, Whitworth & Co. (UK) artillery equipment—part of the global push to modernize field guns with recoil control and improved mobility. Armstrong’s quick-firing carriages incorporated hydro-spring recoil systems and were paired with small-caliber QF guns (6–12 pounders) for use by several European armies. The U.S. Board of Ordnance and Fortification evaluated this carriage to compare it with domestic designs. The action “inspected” suggests the Board examined it but did not adopt it. The U.S. instead developed the 3-inch M1902 field gun, but Armstrong’s designs provided an important benchmark. Surviving Armstrong QF field guns with their carriages are preserved at the Royal Armouries (UK) and in military museums in Japan and Italy.
Proposer Details: Capt. Edmund Louis Zalinski (1849–1909), U.S. Army artillery officer and inventor, submitted the Armstrong carriage for Board evaluation. Zalinski was already known for his invention of the pneumatic dynamite gun in the 1880s, which had been deployed in U.S. coastal defenses. By 1901–1902, he served on or consulted with the Board, sponsoring foreign designs for comparative trials. Although the dynamite gun was later declared obsolete, Zalinski remained an influential figure in experimental ordnance.
Recommendation Reasoning: how the U.S. benchmarked its artillery against leading foreign manufacturers
reasoning_text: how the U.S. benchmarked its artillery against leading foreign manufacturers

Subject: Pneumatic gun carriage
Proposed By: Pneumatic Gun Carriage and Power Co.
Subject Details: Experimental artillery mounting powered by compressed air, developed in the late 1880s and 1890s as part of trials to apply pneumatic systems to naval and turret machinery. These systems were largely experimental and did not see wide adoption. No known extant examples of a pneumatic gun carriage remain in museums or archives (the concept evolved into conventional hydro-pneumatic recoil systems used in later artillery)
Proposer Details: late-19th-century American arms manufacturer incorporated in West Virginia in 1897 with a capital stock of one million dollars, divided into 100,000 shares at ten dollars each. The firm sought to commercialize experimental artillery carriages that employed compressed air (pneumatic power) to manage recoil and improve firing stability, a concept closely related to contemporary “dynamite gun” experiments pioneered by Edmund Zalinski. The company raised funds through stock offerings, as shown by surviving share certificates, and presented its designs for Army testing in the early 1900s.
Recommendation Reasoning: Without verifiable external sources, a deeper dive may yield little. Unless internal archival materials exist, time may be better spent on better-documented experiments.

Subject: Pouch for first-aid packet
Subject Details: A pouch meant to carry the first-aid packet—typically the Carlisle dressing (a linen gauze pad with long tails in a sealed brass casing), standard U.S. Army issue since around 1906. It addressed the need for immediate wound dressing in the field, tied to the broader move from black powder-era logistics to modern battlefield medical readiness. This was a modification of existing medical kit design, not a novel concept or foreign import.
Proposer Details: Proposed by Maj. J. Van R. Hoff, presumably serving in the U.S. Army Medical Department around the early 1900s. He probably recommended appropriating funds or allotting resources for testing this pouch design.
Recommendation Reasoning: medically practical, the pouch itself was an incremental adaptation of existing kits
reasoning_text: medically practical, the pouch itself was an incremental adaptation of existing kits

Subject: Mountain gun
Subject Details: A mountain gun is a lightweight artillery piece designed for use in rugged terrain where traditional guns aren’t practical. In this case, Bethlehem Steel Co. developed a 3-inch Mountain Gun, Model of 1917, intended for such conditions. It was a domestic modification tailored to U.S. Army needs. The U.S. Army made provisions to test it, indicating interest—but it did not enter widespread service. A surviving photograph from September 1918 confirms the model existed.
Proposer Details: Proposed by Bethlehem Steel Company, a major U.S. arms manufacturer in the early 20th century. They were well-known for producing large-caliber guns, howitzers, and forgings for military use. This mountain gun reflects their efforts to diversify artillery offerings. While Bethlehem Steel had broad influence—e.g., M1902 3-inch seacoast guns and M1908 6-inch howitzers—the mountain gun was a less successful niche instrument.
Recommendation Reasoning: small-scale experiment with limited adoption. Unless further documentation surfaces, it’s probably not worth deep investigation relative to more impactful entries.
reasoning_text: small-scale experiment with limited adoption. Unless further documentation surfaces, it’s probably not worth deep investigation relative to more impactful entries.

Subject: Bridle bit
Subject Details: A bridle bit is standard horse tack used to control mounted animals; it’s part of a bridle and typically applies pressure to a horse’s mouth to guide direction and speed. The Army considered purchasing one designed or promoted by Pollock Barber, but ultimately did not recommend it. This would have been a minor modification of existing equestrian equipment, not a new invention or foreign product. Bridle bits were already in common use by this time; many period examples survive in museum collections. No version of Barber’s proposal appears to have been tested or adopted by the U.S. military.
Proposer Details: Pollock Barber proposed the bridle bit for consideration—but there’s no further information available on his background, affiliation

Subject: Nonfulminate fuse
Subject Details: Nonfulminate fuses were proposed as safer, more stable alternatives to traditional mercury fulminate–based detonators, which were highly sensitive to shock and temperature—posing risks during storage, handling, and transport. These newer fuses aimed to replace unstable fulminates with compounds like lead azide or lead styphnate, marking a significant shift in detonation safety. Although Prof. F. Ant. Hubbuch’s design was not adopted, it aligned with a gradual global move toward safer primary explosives—a change that laid the foundation for modern military detonators.
Proposer Details: Prof. F. Ant. Hubbuch appears in Board records as the proposer of a nonfulminate fuse. No further biographical or patent information is currently available, suggesting he may have been an independent researcher or academic whose proposal did not enter broader ordnance documentation.

Subject: "Military gaff"
Subject Details: A gaff is ordinarily a hooked pole or implement, often used in sailing or handling. In a military context, Sovy’s “gaff” was likely conceived as a specialized pole-arm or hook device—perhaps for scaling, handling obstacles, or seizing objects in field or naval operations. The Board noted the proposal but marked it “not recommended.” There is no record of tests, adoption, or surviving examples, suggesting it was considered impractical.

Subject Details: A fire-control system is designed to help weapon systems track/ hit moving targets with greater accuracy. Proposer Details: In 1903, Brigadier General Wallace Randolph served as the Chief of Artillery. That same year marked a major reorganization of the U.S. Army, which split the artillery branch into two components: field artillery and coast artillery.

Subject: Recoil spade, test of
Subject Details: The recoil spade was a mechanical solution used on early artillery to anchor the carriage and absorb recoil, preventing the gun from rolling backward after firing. It was a modification of existing systems, commonly used before hydro-pneumatic recoil mechanisms became widespread.

Subject: Machine-gun carriage
Subject Details: I presume this is in reference to the metallic carriage for the Gatling Gun, Models of 1903. It was a purpose-built, wheeled mount designed to provide mobility, stability, and field adaptability for the rapid-fire weapon. The carriage and limber allowed the gun to be quickly moved across varied terrain and positioned for combat use.
Proposer Details: Lt. John Henry Parker and his team created the portable machine-gun carriage for the Colt Model 1895 Gatling. This allowed for rapid, mobile fire support during the Spanish–American War. Parker designed the carriages and organized his own detachment. His innovative use of machine guns marked the first time the U.S. Army employed them offensively to support infantry in battle.
Recommendation Reasoning: This innovation represents a turning point in the U.S. military, marking the first integration of automatic weapons in offensive operations. His innovations prefigured modern combined arms tactics. A deeper dive would shed light not only on early machine-gun technology but also on the individuals who reshaped military thinking ahead of their time.

Nowadays, high-explosive shells remain common in artillery forces worldwide and are widely preserved in historical collections.

Subject: Langley aerodrome, report
Subject Details: Langley’s Aerodrome was a full-scale, man-carrying airplane built in 1903. The aircraft’s structurally weak, aerodynamically unsound design crashed in both Army-funded launch attempts and was never adopted. Only its engine and partial airframe are at the Smithsonian.

Subject: Projectile with delay-action fuse
Subject Details: Delay-action fuse is a time-delay fuse: after a shell hits its target, a small part inside breaks a moment later, setting off the main explosive only after the shell has had time to dig in. This was meant to stop shells from bursting too soon on impact. The idea fed into the delay fuzes now used in armor-piercing and bunker-busting munitions; surviving examples are mostly in technical collections, not museums.

Subject: Wireless telegraph apparatus
Subject Details: The wireless telegraph apparatus used separate high-frequency “tone” circuits, each corresponding to a distinct signal frequency, to allow the transmission of multiple messages without interference. It also employed a coherer detector. This is a sensitive early radio-wave detector that responded to incoming electromagnetic pulses by altering its conductivity. It was able to translate wireless signals into readable Morse code.

Subject: Smooth bore rotating cannon
Subject Details: A smoothbore weapon has a smooth inside barrel without rifling and fires projectiles without significant spin. The standard infantry arm was the smoothbore musket in the 18th century, with rifled muskets becoming the norm until the middle of the 19th century after the introduction of the Minié ball. The U.S. adopted rifled small arms and artillery during the American Civil War; however, the heavy coastal artillery used Rodman smoothbores until 1900, as newer coastal defense systems like Parrott rifles were still unreliable.

Subject: Modified Lewis range finder
Proposed By: U.S. Rapid Fire Gun and Power Co.
Subject Details: The Lewis rangefinder was a successful invention developed by Colonel Isaac Newton Lewis and was adopted by the War Department in 1896. Seventeen years later, he attempted to improve this range and position finders and ended up with the primary model, excluding all others and saving the government money. He also interestingly designed the Lewis Gun, in 1911, which was the first light machine gun used during WWI. Even though it was designed in the United States, it was not adopted by the U.S. military first because of political differences between Lewis and General William Crozier. Lewis ended up leaving the U.S. Army.
Proposer Details: The U.S. Rapid Fire Gun and Power Co. acquired the Driggs-Seabury Weapons plant in Derby after 1904. The plant manufactured motor vechiles but sold its weapons production to Savage Arms in 1915 and merged comapnies. Under this new merged company, the plant made Lewis guns during WWI. Interestingly, they are proposing this modified Lewis Range finder as far back as 1904, when they had not started making these guns and had acquired this weapons plant.
Recommendation Reasoning: Isaac Newton Lewis, who invented the Lewis Rangefinder and later the Lewis guns, seems to have an intricate and influential relationship to important military inventions before and during World War I. Subject: Wire-cutting projectile
Subject Details: Wire obstacles became prominent in WWI and WWII, used to entangle troops and tactically guard specific areas. A wire-cutting projectile is likely a technology that effectively cuts through these new wire obstacles efficiently. Projectiles like the one Marcianus Rossi designed and patented in 1917, which involved a hollow projectile with cutters inside that could be discharged from the gun and cut through the wires when fired, were submitted but were not widely implemented or successful. More successful methods of overcoming barbed wire included the later development of tanks.

Subject: Caseless cartridge
Subject Details: This invention attempts to eliminate the cartridge case holding the material for the weapon in order to reduce the weight and cost of ammunition. An early type of caseless ammunition was first developed in the 18050s and never saw widespread acceptance. During WWII, the Germans had some success in modernizing the technology, but Japan ultimately developed an aircraft that used internal propellant caseless ammunition. This technology was replaced with external-propellant caseless ammunition, which didn't appear in production until 1968.

Subject: Hollow bullet
Subject Details: A hollow-point bullet expands upon impact with a soft target so that more of the projectile's energy is transported into the target over a smaller distance. Soft leaf bullets would often deform and provide some expansion when they hit a target at a high velocity. At the Hague Convention of 1899, these bullets that can easily expand or flatten were banned; however, the U.S. did not agree to this convention and could use these bullets in warfare, although they did employ a convention to not create arms that cause "unnecessary suffering". Despite the ban in the military, they are some of the most common bullets used by civilians, largely due to the fact that they reduce the risk of bystanders being hit.

Subject: Automatic gun
Proposed By: V. P. De Knight Gun Co.
Subject Details: A firearm that continuously fires when the trigger is activated by using excess energy released from previous discharge to feed new ammunition into the chamber. A fully automatic gun will fire continuously as long as the trigger is depressed and there is ammunition left in the chamber. The mid-1800s, with the invention of the Gatling gun, was the first time a practical weapon could be reloaded relatively quickly. The development of the Maxim machine gun was the first standard automatic gun used in large-scale and modernized warfare.
Proposer Details: The company was founded by Victor P De Knight. He submitted a patent in September 1900 for an automatic rapid-fire gun, which was approved in September 1902.
Recommendation Reasoning: Automatic guns are a crucial invention in defining modern warfare. The ability to rapidly fire machine guns changed WWI tactics, as advancing to enemy lines in groups of people was no longer an effective tactic because they could be shot down so quickly. It would be important to understand particularly how the U.S. developed and the production rates of automatic arms from the U.S. to see whether the Ordnance Board and U.S. manufacturing were a leading player in the forefront of this new technology.

Subject: System of sighting small arms
Subject Details:
Most small arms still used mechanical long-gun iron sights, often with adjustable rear notches calibrated for elevation and windage.

Aperture (peep) sights began gaining traction in U.S. and British service rifles, offering faster sighting and improved accuracy.

Optical sights such as reflector and collimator models had been invented around 1900 but were still rarely used on small arms until aircraft and anti-aircraft roles emerged later.
Recommendation Reasoning: Sights allowed for faster target acquisition and better low-light shooting due to the human eye’s natural ability to center objects in a circle. Became a defining feature of U.S. service rifle.

Subject Details: The U.S. Army designated the Driggs‑Seabury weapon as a semi‑automatic variant of the 6‑pounder Mk XI, used both on naval ships and in limited coastal artillery roles. These guns were mounted on parapet or rampart mounts at coastal forts or aboard vessels like USS Olympia, supporting rapid defense against torpedo boats and small attackers. It featured a breech-action that ejected spent cases automatically, then awaited manual chambering of the next round—streamlining reloading. This semi-automatic function differentiated it from purely manual breech-loading designs and improved rate of fire over earlier Armament.
Proposer Details: Founded around 1897 by U.S. Navy officers William H. Driggs and Samuel Seabury, alongside Louis Labadie “L. L.” Driggs, the company emerged from the earlier Driggs‑Schroeder Ordnance Company and later reorganized as Driggs‑Seabury Ordnance Corporation. Driggs‑Seabury produced key U.S. Army and Navy artillery designs, notably: The 3‑inch M1898 rapid-fire gun (15-pounder), installed on a distinctive masking-parapet (disappearing) carriage. Approximately 120 units were manufactured, later forming the basis for the U.S. M1918 anti-aircraft gun. Rapid-fire Naval 6-pounder and 3-pounder guns (Marks II, III, and Mark 13), supplied to both Army and Navy for coastal defense roles.

Subject: High explosive.
Subject Details: High explosives are materials that detonate—meaning the reaction front travels faster than the speed of sound, producing a supersonic shockwave. This contrasts with low explosives, which deflagrate or burn, with the reaction front moving slower than the speed of sound. During the period around 1900–1907, high explosives typically referred to powerful compounds used in artillery shells and demolition charges, such as: Picric acid (sold as Lyddite in Britain, Melinite in France, Shimose in Japan) and TNT (trinitrotoluene)—not yet universally adopted in U.S. service but gaining attention for its stability.
Recommendation Reasoning: Militaries were moving from black powder to far more powerful shell-filling materials like picric acid and TNT, which offered vastly greater destructive power.

Built upon Allen's collaboration with Major George Owen Squier in creating first wireless telegraph link in Western Hemisphere.

The military relevance in 1907-1908 was significant as By 1907 the value of searchlights had become widely recognized. Subject: Electrically charged wire entanglements
Subject Details: First attempt to weaponize electrical current for area denial warfare, predating electric fence technology by decades. System would use available 110-220V DC military power to electrify barbed wire barriers for perimeter defense of military installations and harbor fortifications. Required sophisticated insulation and power distribution systems with early electrical infrastructure. The concept predated WWI usage, as In 1915, Germany built what was perhaps the first electrified barbed-wire fence to seal off an international boundary: a 125-mile barrier that separated Belgium from the Netherlands. The military had already recognized barbed wire's defensive value - British Army manuals of the 1880s told how to string barbed wire around perimeters and the wicked stuff was used in the Spanish-American, Boer, and Russo-Japanese wars around the turn of the 20th century

Subject: Wire-wound guns, adoption not recommended
Subject Details: Wire-wound or wire-wrapped guns are built-up gun barrels (especially in large/calibre/field/coastal artillery) in which one or more core steel tubes (or “A-tubes”) are tightly wound with high-strength steel wire (sometimes several layers). Then a jacket (outer tube) is fitted over the wire winding. The idea is that the wire gives radial compression / pre-stress, which helps the barrel resist the internal gas pressures when firing without requiring excessively massive forged. The construction method was seen as a way to handle higher pressures or reduce weight vs older “hoop” or monobloc or forged-tube methods, especially at a time when steel manufacture / forgings had limits.
Proposer Details: Brown Wire Gun Company was incorporated in Maine in 1903, representing one of several companies attempting to develop alternative artillery construction methods during the period of major coastal artillery modernization following the Spanish-American War. The Brown Wire Gun Company held patents for “segmental-tube wire-wound” guns (at least a 6-inch and a 10-inch version) which were tested under U.S. War Department / Board of Ordnance & Fortification experimental trials.

Subject Details: Steam-powered vehicle for heavy artillery transport and positioning, providing mechanical power for moving large coastal defense guns and equipment. The traction engine offered superior pulling power compared to horses and enabled transport of extremely heavy artillery pieces across difficult terrain during fort construction and gun emplacement.
Proposer Details: Meinrad Rumely was a German immigrant who founded a blacksmith and foundry business in La Porte, Indiana (mid-19th century). The company evolved over time, producing threshing machines, stationary steam engines, portable engines, and eventually steam traction engines beginning around 1895.  Later, through acquisitions and reorganizations, the firm became Advance-Rumely (1915 onward) and added internal combustion tractors (e.g., OilPull line) along with continuing their steam traction line for some time
